context.description: A philosophy of consciousness chapter on emotional cascades, arguing that feelings are ancient, largely unconscious systems tied to reward, avoidance, and pattern recognition. It explains how humans feel emotions without knowing their origins or controlling their triggers, then introduces a fear-based example involving a gun threat.
normalization_notes: Translated the German chapter heading and prose into English control language.
Assigned consciousness-focused chapter classification from the visible content.

fields.section_heading: The Wonderful World of the Hidden Supercomputer – Emotional Cascades
fields.title_text: The Wonderful World of the Hidden Supercomputer – Emotional Cascades

rows.heading_text: The Wonderful World of the Hidden Supercomputer – Emotional Cascades
rows.text_content: The Wonderful World of the Hidden Supercomputer – Emotional Cascades

rows.paragraph_text: Humans value feelings deeply and seek positive emotions while avoiding pain and danger. The emotional system is described as ancient, largely unconscious, and closely linked to pattern recognition and reward.
The text stresses that people know what they feel but not why they feel it, and that emotional cascades and their triggers remain mostly beyond conscious control.
Examples include fear and disgust toward a spider, affection for a puppy, and a sudden gun threat used to illustrate how rapidly the emotional system reacts.

content.free_text: book_page consciousness_and_mind philosophy_of_consciousness Book page from Bewusstseinsreisen - Version 2 on emotional cascades, ancient feeling systems, unconscious triggers, fear, disgust, and rapid threat response.
